nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reefs among live coral or coral rubble; reported to hide near the base of small live coral heads, especially Pocillopora meandrina. Stays close to the substrate among complex rockwork.

decorPreferences: Plenty of live rock with caves and crevices for retreat and foraging
Fine-sand substrate (~5 cm) — has been documented burying to sleep, similar to other Pseudocheilinus
Tightly-fitting lid or mesh cover — known jumper

diet.notes: Generally accepts a wide range of meaty foods including vitamin-enriched mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, marine pellets and flake. Has been documented actively foraging for small crustaceans, bristleworms, flatworms, and pyramidellid snails on live rock. Best fed in several small meals per day, especially when newly introduced.

goodWith: Hardy reef-safe species of similar or larger size
avoidWith: Other Pseudocheilinus wrasses (Six Line, Eight Line, Scarlet Pin Stripe)
Ornamental shrimp (large specimens may prey on them)

summary: The Fourline Wrasse (Pseudocheilinus tetrataenia) is a small, secretive Pacific reef fish valued for its four vivid horizontal stripes and its usefulness against pest invertebrates such as flatworms and pyramidellid snails. It tends to be semi-aggressive toward other wrasses.
careNotes:
The Fourline Wrasse (Pseudocheilinus tetrataenia) is a small Pacific reef fish closely related to the better-known Six Line Wrasse (Pseudocheilinus hexataenia). The two are sometimes confused in casual writing, but they are distinct species: the Fourline carries four longitudinal stripes rather than six and reaches a noticeably smaller adult size, typically around 6–8 cm. It is native to the Pacific Ocean from Japan south to the Austral Islands and east to the Hawaiian and Tuamotu Islands, where it inhabits seaward reefs from roughly 6 to 44 m depth, staying close to live coral heads and coral rubble.

The Fourline Wrasse is generally considered reef-safe with corals and most ornamental invertebrates, though larger specimens have been reported to hunt small shrimp and other delicate crustaceans. Like its relatives in the genus, it is commonly valued as a natural forager against pest invertebrates, actively hunting pyramidellid snails, flatworms, and bristleworms across the live rock.

On introduction, the species tends to be cryptic and shy, often disappearing into the rockwork for days before settling into routine patrols. A fine-sand bed of around 5 cm is generally recommended, as the fish has been documented burying itself in sand to sleep — behavior typical of the Pseudocheilinus genus.

The Fourline Wrasse is widely regarded as semi-aggressive, particularly toward other members of the same genus (including the Six Line, Eight Line, and Scarlet Pin Stripe Wrasses), as well as fairy and flasher wrasses, leopard wrasses, dottybacks, firefish, and other small, timid fish. The standard guidance is to add this species LAST, after quieter tankmates have already settled in and claimed territory. It generally coexists without difficulty alongside tangs, angelfish, and larger clownfish. Conspecific aggression is considered high, and only one Fourline Wrasse is generally recommended per tank.

Like most members of the wrasse family (Labridae), the Fourline is an able jumper. A tightly-fitting lid or mesh cover with no large openings is considered essential.
breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawning is presumed as in other Pseudocheilinus. Captive breeding is not commonly reported in the aquarium hobby.

primary.notes: Designated primary URL (/products/fourline-wrasse) returned 404; swapped to the alternative LiveAquaria product page (product/6748) which carries the same Four Line Wrasse listing. Used for care level, temperament, water parameters, diet, reef-safe status, and tankmate guidance.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria/misc), max length (7.5 cm TL), depth range (6–44 m), preferred temperature (24.5–28.3°C), native distribution, and oviparous-with-pairing reproductive mode.

additional.name: FishKeeper UK
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (7.5 cm), temperature (25–28°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), jumping risk, and semi-aggressive temperament toward other Pseudocheilinus, dottybacks, grammas, and fairy/flasher/leopard wrasses.

additional.name: Aquatic Community
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), adult size (7.5 cm), sand-burying behaviour, jumping, and aggression toward small fish and delicate inverts. Stated minimum of 20 US gal (~76 L) noted but tank size biased larger per playbook guidance.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank minimum (150 L), reef-safe status, diet (small crustaceans, zooplankton), and aggression notes. Tank-size spread (76 L vs 150 L vs 'XXL') resolved to 150 L per playbook guidance.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner with distinct pairing; not commonly bred in captivity.
